concept_name: Newton's Second Law — Direction Matters (F = m·a as a Vector Equation)
source_book: DC Pandey Class 11 Ch.8 Laws of Motion §8.4 (Newton's Second Law) — table-of-contents reference only; teaching authored from first principles. Phase 0 validation demo Sim 2 (session 56-plan, authored session 59, rewritten to friction gold-standard idiom session 59 round 2). The atomic concept being taught is *direction*: F is a vector, a is a vector, m is a scalar, so a points where F points — NOT where v points. v evolves over time from a. This is the most-confused concept in mechanics: stationary objects can have non-zero forces (balanced), velocity does not equal force, and mass scales the response not gates the existence of motion.

real_world_anchor.primary: Picture a wooden block on a freshly polished marble floor in your school physics lab — friction is almost zero. You push the block with a steady horizontal force. The block does NOT move at constant speed — it speeds up. The harder you push, the faster it speeds up. A heavier block speeds up slower. The acceleration vector points exactly along the force vector. F = m·a is this rule, sharpened into a vector equation that respects direction.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Hockey puck on a wet polished tile floor — push it with a flick. After the flick, no force, the puck drifts at constant speed (Newton I). Now flick it sideways while it is moving — the puck does NOT reverse, and it does NOT keep its original direction. The path CURVES because the new push gives it acceleration in a NEW direction, while velocity is still in the OLD direction. Velocity follows from acceleration over time. They are not the same thing.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Cricket ball hit at 30° upward by the bat. During contact the bat applies F at 30°, so the ball's acceleration is at 30°. After contact, gravity takes over — a different F, pointing straight down — so the acceleration switches direction even though the ball's velocity is still partly upward. At every instant, a points along F_net. The velocity vector evolves from there.

formulas.newton_second_law: F_net = m · a (vector equation — direction matters)
formulas.component_form: a_x = F_x / m, a_y = F_y / m (each component independently)
formulas.velocity_from_acceleration: v(t) = v_0 + a · t (from rest: v(t) = a · t)

constraints: F = m·a holds for the NET force on the body — sum of all forces, not just the applied one
a points along F_net at every instant; v does NOT in general — v is the time-integral of a
Mass is a scalar — it scales magnitude only, never changes direction
If F_net = 0, then a = 0 (Newton I as a special case); v can still be non-zero (constant motion)
Each component of F maps to the same-named component of a independently (a_x = F_x/m, a_y = F_y/m)

STATE_1.title: Push the Block — Constant Push, What Does It Do?

scene_composition.text:
You push with a constant force.
Does the block...
  (a) stay still?
  (b) slide at fixed speed?
  (c) speed up?

tts_sentences.text_en: A wooden block sits on a smooth marble floor. Friction is almost zero.
You push it horizontally with a steady ten newton force.
Does the block stay still, slide at fixed speed, or speed up?

STATE_2.title: Newton II — F = m·a, Solve for Acceleration

scene_composition.text:
F = m · a

10 N = 2 kg · a
a = 10 / 2 = 5 m/s²

Constant push → constant acceleration.
The block speeds up steadily.

tts_sentences.text_en: Newton's second law gives the answer. Force equals mass times acceleration.
Solve for acceleration. Acceleration is force divided by mass.
Ten newtons divided by two kilograms gives five metres per second squared.
Constant push gives constant acceleration. The block speeds up steadily — answer is c.

STATE_3.title: Direction Matters — Push at an Angle

scene_composition.text:
F = m · a is a VECTOR equation.

F is a vector.
m is a scalar (mass = 2 kg).
Dividing a vector by a scalar
preserves direction.

→ a points where F points.
Mass changes the LENGTH of a, never its DIRECTION.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now push the block at an angle — thirty degrees above horizontal.
Quick prediction — where does the acceleration vector point?
Acceleration points exactly along the force, also at thirty degrees.
Force is a vector. Mass is a scalar. Dividing a vector by a scalar preserves direction.
Mass changes the length of the acceleration arrow, never its direction.

STATE_4.title: Velocity Builds Up Over Time — v(t) = a · t

scene_composition.text:
v(t) = a · t

t = 0 s   →  v = 0 m/s
t = 1 s   →  v = 5 m/s
t = 2 s   →  v = 10 m/s
t = 3 s   →  v = 15 m/s

v is NOT F. v is NOT a.
v is what acceleration BUILDS UP over time.

tts_sentences.text_en: Acceleration is constant at five metres per second squared. Watch the block slide.
As time passes, velocity grows. Same direction as acceleration, but increasing in magnitude.
After one second, v is five. After two seconds, v is ten. v of t equals a times t.
Velocity is what acceleration accumulates over time. v is not F. v is not a. v is the build-up.

STATE_5.title: Case A — F aligned with v (the trajectory stays STRAIGHT)

scene_composition.text: CASE A — Force aligned with velocity. What does the trajectory look like?
← past positions (block was here)

F ∥ v
→ a ∥ v
→ ONLY |v| grows
   (direction unchanged)

Trajectory: STRAIGHT LINE

v_x grows (F drives it)
v_y stays 0 (no F_y)

Result: speed up,
no turn.
Next: Case B — what if F is PERPENDICULAR to v?

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is Case A. A block is sliding rightward on a smooth floor. The faded copies behind it are its earlier positions, so you can see it really is moving.
Now we apply a constant force F also pointing rightward. Force is aligned with velocity.
Acceleration follows force. So acceleration also points rightward, parallel to velocity.
What does the block do? It speeds up. The velocity arrow gets longer and longer — but in the same direction.
Only the magnitude of v grows. Direction is unchanged. The trajectory is a straight line.
Next, watch what happens when F is perpendicular to v instead. That is the projectile case — and the trajectory will not stay straight.

STATE_6.title: Case B — Projectile (cannon launch, ball goes UP, peaks, falls DOWN)

scene_composition.text: CASE B — Force is GRAVITY (perpendicular to v at first). Watch the ball rise, peak, and fall.
cannon launch
PEAK: v_y = 0
v_x is CONSTANT throughout flight

v_x: stays SAME
v_y: UP → 0 (peak) → DOWN
F: ALWAYS DOWN (gravity)

Trajectory: PARABOLA

No F_x → v_x stays
a_y = -g → v_y changes

This is projectile motion.
F = m·a, NEVER F = m·v.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now Case B — the most important demo in mechanics. A cannon launches a ball upward and to the right.
Watch the full arc. The ball rises, slows, peaks, then falls back to the ground. The faded copies show five snapshots along the way — at launch, going up, the peak, coming down, and landing.
The blue arrow on the ball is its current velocity. Every moment, it points along the direction of motion — tilting up first, level at peak, tilting down on the way back.
The red arrow is the only force acting — gravity. It points straight down. It never changes direction. Not when the ball is going up, not at the peak, not on the way down.
Acceleration follows force. So acceleration also points straight down — through the entire flight.
Now look at the components. While going up, the ball has horizontal v_x and an upward v_y. Both contribute to the slanted velocity.
At the peak, the upward v_y has become exactly zero. Only horizontal v_x remains. The ball is momentarily moving sideways only.
Coming down, v_x is still the same — gravity has not touched it. But v_y is now downward and growing.
Notice the pattern. The horizontal v_x is the same length at every moment of flight. Why? Because there is no horizontal force, so no horizontal acceleration, so v_x cannot change.
The vertical v_y is where gravity does all its work. v_y goes from upward, to zero at the peak, to downward. That is the complete fingerprint of constant downward gravity.
This is exactly projectile motion. Every thrown ball, every kicked football, every shot arrow traces this same parabolic curve. F equals m a — never F equals m v.

STATE_7.title: Try It Yourself — Vary Force, Mass, and Angle

scene_composition.text_expr: Direction of a: {theta_F.toFixed(0)}° (always parallel to F)
After t = 1 s:  v = {(F / m).toFixed(2)} m/s along F

tts_sentences.text_en: Now you drive. Slide F up to make the acceleration arrow grow.
Slide m up to make the same force produce a smaller acceleration.
Slide theta to rotate the force. Notice acceleration always rotates with it. Direction matters.

epic_c_branches.misconception: If a body is at rest, no force acts on it (or no net force CAN act on it)
trigger_phrases: block is stationary so no force
if it's not moving force is zero
F must be zero if a is zero

STATE_1.title: The Wrong Belief — Rest Means No Force

scene_composition.text:
MYTH: Stationary book
→ zero force on it

Two real forces act:
  weight mg downward
  normal N upward

They BALANCE. Net F = 0.
But individual F values are NOT zero.
Newton II says a = 0, not F = 0.

tts_sentences.text_en: A book sits on a table — clearly not moving. A common student conclusion: no force acts on it.
But weight pulls it down, and the table pushes it up. Two real, non-zero forces.
They cancel. Net force is zero, so acceleration is zero, so the book stays at rest. That is what Newton II predicts.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Velocity points where the force points (or velocity equals force times something)
trigger_phrases: if I push right block moves right immediately

STATE_1.title: The Wrong Belief — v Follows F Directly

scene_composition.text:
MYTH: Apply F, get v immediately
         in the same direction.

F = m·a, NOT F = m·v.

F gives ACCELERATION (rate of change).
v builds up over TIME from a.

At t = 0, F is on but v = 0.
v does not jump — it grows.

Pendulum at the bottom of its swing:
net F = 0, but v is at maximum.
v ≠ F, anywhere, ever.

tts_sentences.text_en: Many students write F equals m v. That is the wrong formula.
Newton II says F equals m a. Force gives acceleration — the rate of change of velocity, not velocity itself.
A pendulum at the lowest point has zero net force but maximum velocity. v and F are different quantities.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Heavier objects need more force just to be there or to be at rest — confusing weight with the force-needed-to-accelerate
trigger_phrases: weight equals force needed to push

STATE_1.title: The Wrong Belief — Mass Demands Force

scene_composition.text:
a = 10 m/s²
(jumps fast)

a = 1 m/s²
(creeps)

MYTH: Heavier ⇒ more F
         needed (just to exist)

Same F = 10 N applied to both.
BOTH accelerate.
Mass scales the RESPONSE,
not the requirement.

tts_sentences.text_en: Students often confuse weight with the force-needed-to-push. They are not the same.
Apply the same ten newton force to a one kilogram block and a ten kilogram block.
Both accelerate. The light one fast, the heavy one slow. Mass scales the response, not the requirement.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Writing F = m·v instead of F = m·a (formula slip with deep conceptual cost — m·v is momentum, not force)
trigger_phrases: newton second law is F = mv

STATE_1.title: The Wrong Formula — F = m·v is Momentum, Not Newton II

scene_composition.text: MYTH: Newton II says F = m·v

Dimensions:
  [F]    = M · L · T⁻²
  [m·v]  = M · L · T⁻¹    ✗
  [m·a]  = M · L · T⁻²    ✓

Only m·a has the dimensions of force.

m · v has a name: it's MOMENTUM (p).
p = m · v.

Newton II actually says:
   F = dp/dt   (rate of change of momentum)
When m is constant:
   dp/dt = m · dv/dt = m · a
So F = m·a is a special case.

F = m·v is dimensionally and physically wrong.

tts_sentences.text_en: F equals m v is a common slip. m v is not a force — it's momentum.
Check dimensions. Force is mass times length divided by time squared. m v is mass times length per time. Off by a factor of time.
Newton's second law actually says force equals rate of change of momentum. When mass is constant, that simplifies to F equals m a.

STATE_1.description: Identify the body and the applied force F. Draw the FBD with weight, normal, applied F.

STATE_2.description: Apply Newton II in magnitude form: F = m·a → solve for a = F/m with given values.

STATE_3.description: Resolve F into components when at angle: F_x = F cos θ, F_y = F sin θ; a_x = F_x/m, a_y = F_y/m.

STATE_4.description: Use kinematics: v(t) = v_0 + a·t. Substitute initial v and computed a to find v at given t.

STATE_5.description: Numeric answer with correct direction. State both magnitude and angle of v at the requested time.

primitives.text: Step 1: Body — wooden block of mass m = 2 kg on a smooth (frictionless) marble floor.
Forces: weight mg ↓, normal N ↑, applied F at angle θ to horizontal.
Floor smooth ⇒ no friction. Vertical balance: N + F sin θ = mg.
+1 for FBD
Step 2: Newton II in magnitude form — F = m · a.
Solve for acceleration: a = F / m.
For F = 10 N, m = 2 kg: a = 10 / 2 = 5 m/s² (rightward).
+1 for a = F/m
Step 3: F at angle θ. Resolve into components.
F_x = F cos θ, F_y = F sin θ. Then a_x = F_x/m, a_y = F_y/m.
|a| = F/m, direction = θ (a is parallel to F).
+1 for components
Step 4: Kinematics — v(t) = v_0 + a · t. From rest: v_0 = 0.
v(t) = a · t. With a = 5 m/s², after t = 2 s: v = 10 m/s.
v points along a (i.e., along F). v_x = a_x·t, v_y = a_y·t.
+1 for v(t) = at
Step 5: For F = 10 N at θ = 30°, m = 2 kg, t = 2 s, starting at rest:
a = F/m = 5 m/s². v = a·t = 10 m/s. Direction: 30° above horizontal.
+1 for numeric

shortcuts.when: Smooth horizontal surface, one external F. Skip the full FBD if friction is given as zero — go direct to a = F/m.

shortcuts.formula: a_x = F cos θ / m, a_y = F sin θ / m
shortcuts.when: F has a vertical component → a is also tilted. Don't assume motion stays along the floor; check if F sin θ > 0 changes contact (block could lift).

shortcuts.trigger: find v after time t (from rest)
shortcuts.when: Combine Newton II and kinematics in one step. Skip computing a separately.

shortcuts.formula: a = F_net / m (vector sum first, then divide)
shortcuts.when: Net F is the vector sum of all applied forces. a always uses NET F, never one of the individual forces.

edge_cases.behavior: a = 0. Block continues at whatever v it already had (Newton I as a special case).

edge_cases.boundary: F applied but body has friction
edge_cases.behavior: Net F = F_applied - friction (when sliding). a = (F_applied - friction)/m. Use NET force, not just applied.
Block lifts off. Normal becomes zero. Net F = F - mg upward. a = (F - mg)/m upward.
Block stays on surface. Normal adjusts (N = mg - F). Net F = 0 horizontally and vertically. a = 0.

edge_cases.boundary: v non-zero, F perpendicular to v (e.g., gravity on a thrown ball)
edge_cases.behavior: v_perp grows from a, v_parallel unchanged. Trajectory curves. This is exactly projectile motion.
